topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the chipped kitchen counter, a stark white rectangle mocking the cluttered mess of my morning. Liam’s handwriting, usually a chaotic swirl of sharp angles and careless loops, was unnervingly neat on this one. I picked it up, the paper feeling thin and brittle beneath my fingertips. My stomach tightened, a dull, heavy knot forming just beneath my ribs. Across the top, in bold, black ink, was the address: Denver. Three thousand miles. I reread the address three times, the ink blurring slightly as I focused on the unfamiliar geography. I traced the outline of the city on the counter with my finger, a pointless, repetitive motion. I didn't look at my phone; the screen felt too bright, too demanding. Just the silence of the apartment, the hum of the refrigerator, and the heavy realization settling over me like a damp blanket. I finally dropped the letter into the recycling bin, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet room.

[story 2]

I stared out the window, watching the familiar streetlights blur through the damp evening air. Every familiar landmark seemed suddenly distant, painted in hues I hadn't noticed before. When the text arrived—a simple notification with a travel itinerary—my breath hitched. It wasn't a sudden shock; it was a slow, pervasive sinking. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself to the present moment, but the image of Liam packing boxes in a rented truck kept flashing behind my eyes. My shoulders slumped, the tension that had been coiled tight all day finally unraveling into a slow, heavy droop. "Denver," I whispered to the empty room, the sound raspy. I pushed myself up from the sofa, the movement feeling like lifting lead weights. I walked to the bookshelf, running my hand over spines, seeking some solid ground, but the silence in the house amplified the vast, empty space opening up between us.

[story 3]

It was the smell of old coffee and stale air that announced the news. I hadn't even opened the mail initially; I just picked up the stack of junk mail, intending to toss it all at once. But then I saw it—the stark, official-looking notice about Liam’s relocation. My hands started to tremble, a fine, uncontrollable vibration that made gripping the paper difficult. It felt like the world had tilted on its axis. I sank onto the floor, the rug rough against my knees. I didn't cry; the tears seemed too large, too dramatic for the sheer weight of the situation. Instead, I just sat there, breathing in shallow, quick gasps, cataloging the details of the news as if they were foreign objects. Every muscle in my body felt disconnected, heavy and unresponsive. I just kept staring at the scuffed floorboards, waiting for the tremors in my limbs to subside.
